item.title: Shrinkage priors for Bayesian Substitute Confounders
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 50, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, repository quality, public implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in stat.ME, cs.LG, and math.ST.; Abstract explicitly targets deconfounder and substitute-confounder failure modes in multi-cause observational studies.; Claims consistency for mean potential outcomes under stated posterior concentration, contraction, overlap geometry, and latent-variable identification conditions.; Empirical evidence mentioned includes synthetic experiments and an ADNI baseline analysis.

TL;DR: The paper proposes sparse Bayesian substitute confounders for multi-cause observational causal inference, using shrinkage-prior factor assignment models to preserve shared treatment-assignment variation without over-encoding individual causes.

Problem: Multi-cause observational studies may reveal unmeasured confounding through dependence among causes, but existing substitute-confounder approaches can fit the cause distribution while producing adjustment scores that overfit the treatment vector, destroy overlap, or capture single-cause variation.

Method: A Bayesian factor assignment framework learns sparse, low-dimensional substitute confounders with shrinkage priors, then uses regression-adjusted estimators; the theory is framed through posterior concentration, factor-score contraction, and overlap-preserving assignment geometry rather than a specific prior choice.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the use of shrinkage-prior latent factor structure to favor multi-cause-supported factors, discourage effectively single-cause factors, and diagnose factor collapse, addressing known failure modes of flexible deconfounder-style assignment models.

Main result: Synthetic experiments illustrate effects of signal strength, outcome validity, and geometry-aware regularization; an ADNI baseline analysis finds sparse substitute scores recover much of the adjustment obtained by directly conditioning on invasive cerebrospinal-fluid biomarkers.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative effect sizes, baselines, ablations, uncertainty intervals, code availability, or enough detail to judge robustness; consistency still depends on latent-variable identification assumptions.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the causality query because it studies causal adjustment for unmeasured confounding in observational multi-cause settings and directly extends deconfounder/substitute-confounder methodology.

Tags: causality, observational studies, unmeasured confounding, deconfounder, Bayesian factor models, shrinkage priors, multi-cause inference, ADNI

Figure note: A useful figure would show the Bayesian factor assignment pipeline: observed multiple causes feeding a shrinkage-prior latent factor model, sparse substitute scores, overlap/collapse diagnostics, and regression-adjusted causal estimators.

item.title: Causal Forecasting in Panel Data: A Two-Way Synthetic Forecasting Approach
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 50, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliation not supplied, author citation count or h-index not supplied, no DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied, no code repository mentioned, no public data availability mentioned, no numerical empirical results in abstract

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in econ.EM and stat.ME.; Abstract states a new framework for prospective causal forecasting in panel data.; Proposes the Two-Way Synthetic Forecasting estimator, TWSF.; Claims finite-sample forecasting error bounds, pointwise consistency, asymptotic normality, and pointwise inference.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Two-Way Synthetic Forecasting, a framework for estimating prospective causal effects in panel data by combining synthetic-control logic with multivariate time-series forecasting.

Problem: It studies how to estimate what will happen to a target unit under an intervention it has not yet experienced beyond the observed panel, rather than only reconstructing retrospective counterfactuals inside the observed period.

Method: The framework imposes low-rank temporal structure on latent time factors in panel-data latent factor models, then uses a Two-Way Synthetic Forecasting estimator that learns cross-unit relationships from pre-treatment outcomes and combines them with a time-series model learned from donor units that have experienced the intervention.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the prospective causal forecasting setup: it extends synthetic-controls-style counterfactual reasoning from observed-panel retrospective questions to future intervention forecasts with finite-sample error bounds, consistency, inference via orthogonalized correction, and multi-step forecasting extensions.

Main result: The abstract reports finite-sample forecasting error bounds implying pointwise consistency, asymptotic normality for an orthogonalized correction, analogous guarantees for direct and recursive multi-step forecasts, simulation evidence, and an application to NFL stadium openings during the 2020 season.

Limitation: The abstract does not report numerical empirical results, baseline comparisons, code availability, data availability, robustness checks, or detailed simulation design, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it develops methodology for causal effect estimation and inference in panel data, specifically for forecasting intervention effects prospectively.

Tags: causality, panel-data, synthetic-controls, causal-forecasting, time-series, policy-evaluation, econometrics

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or schematic showing how TWSF combines pre-treatment cross-unit synthetic-control learning with intervention-side donor time-series forecasting for prospective causal estimands.

item.title: Concept Modulation Models: A Unified Framework for Identifiability and Extrapolation
item.description:
Priority: 42/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 55, Empirical 25, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI or venue signal, open-source code, public data, empirical benchmarks

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in cs.LG and stat.ML.; Abstract introduces Concept Modulation Models with A -> Lambda -> C -> X structure.; Abstract explicitly mentions causal representation learning, nonlinear ICA, and perturbation modeling.; Claims are theoretical: algebraic extrapolation criteria and recovery of prior stated conclusions when combined with model-specific rigidity arguments.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Concept Modulation Models as a unified theoretical framework for identifiability and extrapolation in conditional latent variable models, including causal representation learning settings.

Problem: It studies how conditional latent variable models can reliably identify latent structure from observed attribute variation and extrapolate distributions to unseen attributes.

Method: The paper introduces Concept Modulation Models with structure A -> Lambda -> C -> X, where attributes select modulators, modulators induce latent concept laws, and concepts generate observed features. It uses attribute potentials, defined as log-density ratios between attribute-conditioned concept laws, to separate generic identifiability lifting from model-specific rigidity arguments.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a unifying potential-based framework that connects identifiability and extrapolation guarantees across nonlinear ICA, causal representation learning, perturbation modeling, and related conditional latent variable models, rather than proving each result in a model-specific way.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: feature agreement on observed attributes induces a constrained latent concept transition, and extrapolation to unseen attributes holds exactly when transported attribute-potential identities extend to those attributes. No empirical result is reported in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention experiments, baselines, ablations, code, public data, robustness checks, or concrete worked examples; the claims appear primarily theoretical and depend on model-specific rigidity arguments from prior work.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal representation learning' because it explicitly targets identifiability and extrapolation in conditional latent variable models and names causal representation learning as one of the existing areas unified by the framework.

Tags: causal representation learning, identifiability, extrapolation, conditional latent variable models, generative models, nonlinear ICA, theory

Figure note: A useful figure would show the Concept Modulation Model pipeline A -> Lambda -> C -> X and how attribute potentials transport identifiability and extrapolation constraints across observed and unseen attributes.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper proposes an interpretable causal-discovery-guided Sleep Recovery Score from multimodal polysomnography that aligns patient-reported recovery better than AHI across two cohorts.

Problem: Objective sleep metrics such as AHI summarize PSG findings but may poorly reflect patient-reported outcomes like sleepiness, fatigue, and perceived recovery because they do not capture multidomain physiology.

Method: The authors use DAG learning on multimodal PSG features from MESA and MrOS to identify physiological drivers across respiratory burden, hypoxic burden, sleep fragmentation, sleep architecture, and autonomic regulation, then apply physiology-based constraints plus constrained LLM-assisted auditing to remove structural confounders and construct-overlapping variables before building a hierarchical Sleep Recovery Score.

Key contribution: A domain-structured, bias-aware framework that combines causal discovery with physiological constraints and LLM-assisted auditing to derive an interpretable recovery score rather than relying on a single respiratory index such as AHI.

Main result: Across MESA (n=1540) and MrOS (n=825), the resulting SRS reportedly shows up to 2.5x stronger alignment with perceived recovery than AHI.

Limitation: The abstract does not report statistical uncertainty, detailed baselines beyond AHI, ablations, external clinical validation, code availability, or robustness checks; the LLM-assisted auditing procedure is described only at a high level.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query because causal discovery and DAG learning are central to the proposed framework, applied to a concrete connected-health and clinical sleep modeling problem.

Tags: causal discovery, DAG learning, sleep medicine, polysomnography, patient-reported outcomes, connected health, interpretable ML

item.title: Apportioning Causal Responsibility of Two Risk Factors for an Adverse Outcome via Counterfactual Attribution
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in stat.ME.; Abstract states a quantitative framework for apportioning causal responsibility between two binary risk factors.; Uses latent causal types and potential outcomes under all exposure combinations.; Claims nonparametric identification under no confounding, monotonicity, and a structural balance condition.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a counterfactual, nonparametric framework for retrospectively apportioning causal responsibility between two binary risk factors for an adverse outcome.

Problem: It studies how to assign causal responsibility after an adverse outcome has already occurred, where two binary risk factors jointly contributed and the individual's full latent causal type is unobserved.

Method: The authors define average causal responsibility as expected responsibility over latent causal types defined by potential outcomes under all exposure combinations. Under no confounding and monotonicity, they identify the metric nonparametrically when a structural balance condition holds, and derive sharp bounds when it does not.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is a formal quantitative attribution framework for splitting retrospective causal responsibility between two risk factors, rather than estimating prospective effects of causes in the usual causal-inference setting.

Main result: The abstract claims nonparametric identification under no confounding, monotonicity, and a structural balance condition, plus sharp bounds outside that balance condition. It illustrates the framework with smoking and asbestos exposures in lung cancer.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data validation, baselines, robustness checks, software, or how sensitive the attribution is to violations of no confounding, monotonicity, or the balance condition.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it develops counterfactual attribution methods, potential-outcome definitions, identification results, and bounds for causal responsibility.

Tags: causal inference, counterfactual attribution, causal responsibility, potential outcomes, nonparametric identification, sharp bounds, binary risk factors

item.title: RegimeVGGT: Layer-Wise Spatially Preserving Redundancy Removal for Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer
item.description:
Priority: 45/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 45, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or implementation release mentioned., Datasets and evaluation metrics are not named in the abstract., Ablations, statistical robustness, and failure cases are not described in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV and cs.RO, published 2026-06-16.; Abstract identifies quadratic cross-frame attention in VGGT as the scalability bottleneck.; Abstract claims spectral, probing, and causal analyses reveal shallow, middle, and deep layer regimes.; Method is described as training-free and layer-wise, with saliency-guided token merging and selectively protected K/V downsampling.

TL;DR: RegimeVGGT is a training-free accelerator for VGGT that uses layer-dependent token and K/V compression to reduce multi-view 3D reconstruction cost while preserving geometry and pose quality.

Problem: The paper studies the scalability bottleneck of Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer, where quadratic cross-frame attention makes dense 3D scene recovery from multi-view images expensive.

Method: It proposes RegimeVGGT, a layer-wise compression framework based on spectral, probing, and causal analyses of VGGT layers. The method applies U-shaped compression across layers using Saliency-Guided Banded Merging for geometry- and edge-salient tokens and Selectively Protected K/V Downsampling to preserve cross-frame coverage, reference-frame anchoring, camera/register tokens, and pose-critical attention paths.

Key contribution: The main novelty is the claim that VGGT layers fall into distinct functional regimes and should not be compressed uniformly; RegimeVGGT uses layer-aware, spatially preserving compression along both token and K/V axes without retraining.

Main result: The abstract reports a 6.7x speedup over VGGT* at matched reconstruction quality.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, metrics, baselines beyond VGGT*, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or whether the causal analysis supports broader causal-inference claims.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it mentions causal analyses of transformer layer regimes, but its primary contribution is efficient 3D vision transformer inference rather than causality as a modeling or inference framework.

Tags: computer-vision, 3d-reconstruction, multi-view-geometry, transformer-efficiency, attention-compression, training-free-acceleration, causal-analysis

Figure note: A useful image would show the RegimeVGGT pipeline: layer-regime analysis, U-shaped layer-wise compression, saliency-guided banded token merging, and selectively protected K/V downsampling with preserved camera/register tokens and reference-frame anchor.

item.description:

TL;DR: TSCD is a causal discovery algorithm that uses tensors built from observational and interventional covariance matrices to recover DAG structure and edge functions under linear SEM assumptions, with a nonlinear variant also implemented.

Problem: The paper studies how to recover causal dependencies among variables from observational and interventional data, especially using statistically efficient second-order information rather than higher-order moments.

Method: It proposes Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD), which takes a tensor formed from covariance matrices across observational and interventional settings and, under a linear structural equation model on a DAG with uncorrelated noise variables, outputs both the DAG and edge functions; the authors also report a nonlinear implementation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that second-order covariance information organized as a tensor can identify causal order and parameters with a number of interventions logarithmic in the number of variables, while avoiding assumptions such as non-Gaussianity or higher-order moment availability.

Main result: From the abstract: TSCD is claimed to be robust to noise, competitive with existing methods, and scalable to hundreds of variables; it also has theoretical identifiability with logarithmically many interventions.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, baselines, ablations, metrics, statistical uncertainty, intervention design details, or how broadly the nonlinear version is validated.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causal discovery' by proposing a new causal discovery algorithm with identifiability claims and empirical evaluation for observational plus interventional data.

item.title: SAE Interventions are Unreliable: Post-Intervention Recovery of Suppressed Behavior
item.description:
Priority: 62/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 42, Paper 55, Empirical 75, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent from supplied metadata., Citation count and h-index absent., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No ablation details in abstract., No statistical robustness or confidence intervals in abstract., No dataset sizes or full model coverage details in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 posted 2026-06-16.; Abstract names a constrained residual-space optimization method for post-intervention recovery.; Reports experiments across TPP, unlearning, IOI, and refusal steering.; Reports 95.8% recovery rate on valid refusal-steering samples with defended-feature relative drift 0.131.

TL;DR: The paper argues that sparse-autoencoder feature interventions can suppress an observed unsafe feature while leaving alternative residual-stream paths that recover the same behavior.

Problem: It studies whether SAE-based latent-space defenses provide behavioral control, or merely block one feature-level route to a behavior such as refusal steering, unlearning, IOI, or other targeted behaviors.

Method: The authors formulate post-intervention recovery as a constrained residual-space optimization problem: starting from the residual state after an SAE feature intervention, they optimize perturbations that recover the pre-intervention behavior while preserving the targeted SAE feature values. They use encoder-orthogonal updates for single-layer interventions and a feature-map Jacobian for cross-layer settings to avoid simply undoing the clamp.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a stress test showing that successful feature-level clamping need not imply behavioral completeness; recovery can occur through SAE-unexplained residual components, especially the reconstruction residual.

Main result: In the refusal-steering setting, the abstract reports a 95.8% recovery rate on valid samples while keeping defended-feature relative drift at 0.131, below suffix-based baselines. The paper also reports recoverable behavior across TPP, unlearning, IOI, and refusal-steering experiments.

Limitation: The abstract does not report affiliations, ablations, statistical uncertainty, dataset sizes, model families, full baseline details, or external validation beyond the named experiment types.

Relevance: The paper is highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it directly evaluates whether causal interventions on SAE features actually control downstream behavior, exposing a causal gap between feature-level mechanisms and behavioral outcomes.

Tags: sparse-autoencoders, mechanistic-interpretability, causal-intervention, latent-space-defense, model-safety, residual-stream, refusal-steering

item.title: Fast Nonparametric Conditional Independence Testing via Two-Stage Regression
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 44, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: No affiliation or institution metadata supplied., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No exact empirical metrics, tables, or confidence intervals in the abstract., No ablation or robustness evidence visible in the abstract., No public dataset or benchmark details beyond synthetic graphs and flow-cytometry data.

Score evidence: Abstract explicitly targets constraint-based causal discovery and repeated conditional independence tests.; Method details include polynomial regression, nonlinear feature map, shallow tree residualization, residual cross-covariance, and moment-matched chi-square approximation.; Empirical claims mention simulations, synthetic graphs, flow-cytometry data, and comparisons to fast kernel, random-feature, and regression-based competitors.; Metadata says arXiv v1 and includes an R implementation with C++ back-end at a GitHub URL.

TL;DR: BLITZ is a fast nonparametric conditional independence test for constraint-based causal discovery that combines broad polynomial residualization with local shallow-tree residualization to improve calibration while staying computationally light.

Problem: The paper studies how to perform many conditional independence tests quickly in constraint-based causal discovery without losing null calibration under nonlinear dependence on the conditioning set.

Method: It proposes BLITZ, a two-stage residualization test: first remove broad smooth dependence on the conditioning set using low-order polynomial regression, then apply a small nonlinear feature map and residualize those features with shallow tree regressions before testing residual cross-covariance using a moment-matched chi-square null approximation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is the broad-to-local residualization design, which is argued theoretically to reduce the effective complexity handled by shallow tree residualizers, improving calibration while avoiding overfitting and sub-second runtimes.

Main result: From the abstract, BLITZ has better null calibration than fast kernel, random-feature, and regression-based competitors in simulations, remains among the fastest tested methods, and improves reliability of endpoint orientations in causal discovery experiments on synthetic graphs and flow-cytometry data while maintaining competitive structural recovery.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact quantitative metrics, sample sizes, ablations, robustness checks, or detailed failure cases; the paper is arXiv v1 with no DOI, journal, or conference signal in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query `causal discovery`: conditional independence testing is a core bottleneck in constraint-based causal discovery, and the proposed test is evaluated inside causal discovery workflows.

Tags: causal discovery, conditional independence testing, nonparametric statistics, constraint-based discovery, residualization, stat.ML

Figure note: A useful figure would show the BLITZ pipeline: conditioning-set polynomial residualization, nonlinear feature map construction, shallow-tree residualization, residual cross-covariance statistic, and chi-square null approximation.

item.title: SoftMoE: Soft Differentiable Routing for Mixture-of-Experts in LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 59/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 75, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 55, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals not supplied., DOI and journal reference absent., No quantitative metrics in abstract., No dataset names or benchmark details in abstract., No ablation, robustness, or statistical significance details in abstract.

Score evidence: Abstract identifies the non-differentiability of discrete top-k routing as the core problem.; Proposes truncated soft top-k LapSum relaxation for differentiable expert routing.; Adds learnable mean active experts per layer with a global budget constraint.; Claims comparable or better language modeling and downstream performance while activating fewer experts.

TL;DR: SoftMoE proposes a differentiable soft routing mechanism for autoregressive sparse MoE LLMs that learns non-uniform expert allocation across layers while using fewer active experts than standard sparse MoE.

Problem: The paper studies inefficient and rigid expert usage in sparse Mixture-of-Experts LLMs caused by non-differentiable discrete top-k routing, which fixes the number of active experts per input despite a constrained inference budget.

Method: It replaces discrete top-k routing with a truncated soft top-k LapSum relaxation, parameterizes the mean number of active experts per layer, and applies a global budget constraint so routing and layer-wise expert allocation can be optimized by gradient descent.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a soft differentiable routing framework for autoregressive MoE LLMs that can learn how many experts to activate per layer instead of enforcing a fixed top-k count everywhere.

Main result: From the abstract, SoftMoE reportedly matches or outperforms sparse MoE on language modeling and downstream tasks while activating significantly fewer experts; learned expert allocation is highly non-uniform, with later layers using more experts.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative gains, dataset names, metric values, ablation details, robustness analysis, or the code URL, so the strength and reproducibility of the empirical claim cannot be fully judged from supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query only tangentially: the abstract mentions preserving causality for autoregressive language modeling, but the paper is about MoE routing efficiency rather than causal inference or causality research.

Tags: mixture-of-experts, LLM, differentiable-routing, sparse-computation, autoregressive-modeling, ICML-2026

Figure note: A useful figure would show the SoftMoE routing pipeline: soft top-k LapSum relaxation, global budget constraint, and learned layer-wise expert activation pattern compared with fixed top-k sparse MoE.

item.title: PearlVLA: Progressive Embodied Action-Plan Refinement in Latent Space
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 40, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 62

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index signals, DOI or venue, numerical benchmark results, specific baselines, ablations

Score evidence: arXiv v1 preprint, published 2026-06-16.; Abstract proposes PearlVLA with latent-space iterative plan refinement and a frozen latent world model.; Abstract claims state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO but gives no numerical results.; Metadata lists no DOI, journal reference, affiliations, citation signals, code, or data links.

TL;DR: PearlVLA proposes a VLA framework that performs iterative action-plan deliberation in latent space to improve embodied robot control while preserving low-latency parallel action decoding.

Problem: The paper studies the trade-off in Vision-Language-Action models between fast direct action decoding and slower explicit deliberation methods such as textual reasoning, pixel subgoals, or action search.

Method: PearlVLA separates VLM meta-query representations into a fixed visual-grounding branch and an iterative latent-plan branch; each refinement round uses a plan-conditioned world query to obtain an imagined future observation latent from a lightweight frozen latent world model, then a future-guided RefineNet applies scheduled residual updates before decoding the refined latent plan into an action chunk. It also introduces Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize refinement using rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures.

Key contribution: The genuinely new idea claimed in the abstract is moving deliberative planning into latent VLM representations, combining iterative latent future imagination, residual plan refinement, and process-reward RL while retaining parallel low-latency action generation.

Main result: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance among existing methods on the LIBERO benchmark, but does not provide numerical scores, task breakdowns, or statistical evidence.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative results, baselines, ablations, latency measurements, robustness tests, code availability, or details sufficient to judge whether the causal/process-reward component is essential.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' mainly through its 'Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL' component. It appears more centrally about latent planning for VLA robotics than causal inference, so relevance to a causality-focused feed is moderate to low unless the researcher tracks causal reward assignment or intervention-like latent plan edits.

Tags: vision-language-action, robotics, latent planning, embodied AI, reinforcement learning, LIBERO, causality-adjacent

Figure note: A framework diagram showing the visual-grounding branch, iterative latent-plan refinement loop, latent world model future prediction, RefineNet residual updates, and parallel action-chunk decoder would be useful; metadata indicates 2 figures but provides no direct image URL.

item.title: TIGER: Inverting Transformer Gradients via Embedding-Subspace Distance Optimization
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, conference, or journal venue, code repository, public data or benchmark details, exact metrics and effect sizes

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16; no DOI or journal reference supplied.; Abstract names TIGER and describes a continuous differentiable objective over token embeddings.; Abstract claims experiments on encoder-only and decoder models, with improved reconstruction quality/runtime and DP-defended reconstructions.; Categories are cs.CR, cs.DC, and cs.LG, aligning with ML security and federated learning rather than causality.

TL;DR: TIGER proposes a continuous embedding-subspace optimization attack for reconstructing transformer inputs from federated-learning gradients, improving robustness and runtime over prior token-search or full-gradient-matching attacks according to the abstract.

Problem: The paper studies privacy leakage in federated learning for transformers, where client gradient updates can reveal enough information to reconstruct private inputs. It focuses on weaknesses of prior transformer gradient inversion attacks: expensive and unstable full-gradient matching, and brittle/scaling-limited subspace token membership tests under numerical noise, quantization, DP, and non-causal encoder attention.

Method: TIGER converts the low-rank attention-gradient subspace signal into a differentiable objective. Instead of searching discrete token candidates or matching full gradients, it directly optimizes token embeddings to minimize distance to the inferred embedding subspace.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is replacing brittle discrete token membership testing with continuous embedding-subspace distance optimization, enabling gradient inversion for transformers that is faster for encoder-only models and more robust for decoder models under noisy or DP-defended settings.

Main result: The abstract reports substantial improvements in reconstruction quality and runtime on encoder-only models versus existing attacks, and greater robustness on decoder models, including the first successful reconstructions in DP-defended federated learning settings. Exact datasets, metrics, effect sizes, and baselines are not provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The supplied metadata/abstract does not report code, public data, exact benchmarks, quantitative metrics, ablations, statistical robustness, DP parameters, or affiliations. Claims about empirical strength therefore require checking the full paper.

Relevance: Relevance to the configured query 'causality' appears weak: the match likely comes from the phrase 'non-causal attention' rather than causal inference or causality research. It may still interest researchers tracking transformer internals, privacy, or attention mechanisms.

Tags: federated-learning, gradient-inversion, transformers, privacy-attacks, differential-privacy, embedding-optimization, cs.CR, weak-causality-match

Figure note: A useful figure would show the TIGER pipeline: extracting the attention-gradient subspace, optimizing continuous token embeddings by subspace distance, and decoding/reconstructing inputs, preferably contrasted with discrete token membership testing.

item.title: ActWorld: From Explorable to Interactive World Model via Action-Aware Memory
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in cs.CV.; Abstract identifies data and memory bottlenecks for object interaction in interactive world models.; Claims a 100K interaction video dataset with per-chunk captions.; Introduces hierarchical action-aware memory and persistent memory bank.

TL;DR: ActWorld proposes an interactive world model that adds mid-rollout object interaction to navigation-centric video/world generators using action-aware hierarchical memory and a 100K interaction-video dataset.

Problem: The paper studies the gap between visually explorable world models and genuinely actionable ones: existing interactive world models mostly support navigation actions while failing to preserve and execute object interactions such as opening doors, picking up items, or triggering physical responses over long rollouts.

Method: The abstract describes ActWorld as a chunk-autoregressive interactive world model with a hierarchical action-aware memory mechanism that compresses history based on interaction importance, plus a persistent memory bank for event-update and object-identity tokens. It also constructs a 100K human-object interaction video dataset with per-chunk captions generated via chain-of-thought reasoning.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is shifting interactive world modeling beyond navigation by explicitly addressing two bottlenecks: lack of densely labeled interaction data and memory compression that forgets causally important event-transition frames.

Main result: The abstract reports that ActWorld supports both flexible navigation and rich object interaction in one model, substantially improving interaction fidelity over navigation-only baselines without sacrificing viewpoint control; exact metrics are not provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, dataset release status, code availability, ablations, robustness tests, or details on whether chain-of-thought-derived captions introduce annotation noise.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because its method explicitly targets causal event-transition frames and memory mechanisms that preserve object-state changes across rollouts.

Tags: interactive world models, object interaction, action-aware memory, video generation, human-object interaction, causal memory, cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper develops proximal causal inference methods for identifying and estimating path-specific mediation effects when recanting witnesses are hidden or unknown.

Problem: It studies mediation analysis when treatment-induced mediator-outcome confounders, or recanting witnesses, may exist but are unobserved or not known in advance, undermining standard identification of path-specific effects.

Method: The authors propose three proximal identification strategies, derive a semiparametric efficient influence function, introduce a proximal multiply robust estimator, and use a minimax optimization-based debiased machine learning procedure for estimation and confidence intervals.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element claimed in the abstract is handling hidden or unknown recanting witnesses in mediation analysis using proximal causal inference, rather than assuming such variables are absent or fully specified a priori.

Main result: The abstract reports theoretical guarantees: consistency if at least one nuisance-model set is correctly specified, asymptotic normality and semiparametric efficiency when all nuisance models are correctly specified with suitable rates, plus simulation and real-data demonstrations. No quantitative empirical results are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, baselines, simulation settings, effect sizes, robustness checks, code availability, or how sensitive the method is to proximal proxy assumptions.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it addresses identification and estimation of causal mediation/path-specific effects under hidden confounding using proximal causal inference.

item.title: SpatioTemporal Causal Network Diagnostics for Geographic Tipping Point Early Warning
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 40, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No code repository or implementation release mentioned., No data availability statement mentioned., No ablations, uncertainty intervals, statistical tests, or robustness analysis mentioned in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in cs.LG.; Method uses transfer entropy, dynamic mode decomposition, and three vulnerability signals.; Empirical validation includes synthetic bifurcations plus Indo-Pacific SST and North Atlantic AMOC benchmarks.; AMOC task reports AUROC 0.783 and critical-subnetwork IoU 0.378.

TL;DR: The paper proposes ST-CND, a transfer-entropy and dynamic-mode-decomposition framework for localized early warning of geographic tipping points by modeling spatial fields as time-evolving directed causal networks.

Problem: Localized early warning for geographic tipping points in ecosystems, climate subsystems, and ice sheets, where classical global spatial indicators such as Moran's I can fail under spatial dilution, Euclidean-neighborhood assumptions, and correlated noise.

Method: ST-CND builds a directed information-flow topology using transfer entropy, estimates local recovery rates for candidate subnetworks with dynamic mode decomposition, and ranks vulnerable subnetworks using internal fluctuation, internal synchronization, and low external coupling.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is combining causal network inference with local dynamical recovery diagnostics to replace fixed Euclidean neighborhoods and reduce false alarms from spatially correlated noise.

Main result: Validated on synthetic bifurcations and two observational SST benchmarks; on the North Atlantic AMOC task, ST-CND reports AUROC 0.783 and critical-subnetwork IoU 0.378, outperforming recurrence-network and lambda-AR1 baselines.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, data availability, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness tests, parameter sensitivity, affiliations, citation signals, DOI, or peer-review venue.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the central framework uses transfer entropy to infer directed causal/information-flow networks for spatial early-warning diagnostics.

Tags: causality, transfer-entropy, spatiotemporal-modeling, early-warning-signals, climate-science, dynamic-mode-decomposition, network-diagnostics

Figure note: A useful image would be the ST-CND pipeline diagram showing spatial nodes, transfer-entropy directed edges, candidate subnetworks, DMD recovery-rate estimation, and final vulnerable-subnetwork ranking.

item.title: FoundCause: Causal Discovery with Latent Confounders from Observational Data
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 45, Paper 50, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue in metadata., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No explicit affiliations supplied, aside from an amazon-science GitHub link., No ablation results in the abstract., No statistical significance or confidence intervals reported in the abstract., No dataset names or scenario details in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16.; Abstract claims training entirely on synthetic structural causal models.; Abstract claims explicit latent confounder modeling via learnable latent tokens.; Abstract reports comparisons against 11 classical and 4 amortized causal discovery methods.

TL;DR: FoundCause is an amortized transformer-based causal discovery model trained on synthetic SCMs to infer causal graphs, including latent confounders, from observational data in a single forward pass.

Problem: The paper studies causal discovery from observational data, specifically recovering directed causal structure and latent confounding when interventions are unavailable.

Method: It proposes FoundCause, an amortized causal discovery architecture trained entirely on synthetic structural causal models. The model uses a permutation-invariant transformer with alternating sample/variable attention, statistics-conditioned attention from pairwise asymmetry measures, a factorized edge/direction decoder, triangular motif refinement, masked handling of missing data, and a latent-token confounder module.

Key contribution: The main claimed novelty is explicitly modeling latent confounding within an amortized causal discovery model, while combining learned synthetic-data transfer with classical causal asymmetry features and higher-order motif reasoning.

Main result: According to the abstract, FoundCause outperforms 11 classical non-amortized methods and 4 amortized methods on 15 real-world datasets, with +9.6% F1, +1.2% AUROC, and 18.9% lower structural Hamming distance versus the strongest non-amortized methods, while requiring only one forward pass at inference.

Limitation: The abstract does not report ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness tests, details of the 15 datasets, failure cases, or how synthetic-training assumptions affect real-world generalization.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causal discovery" because it directly targets observational causal graph recovery, compares against PC/GES/NOTEARS-style methods, and focuses on latent confounders, a central challenge in causality.

Tags: causal discovery, latent confounding, observational data, amortized inference, transformer, synthetic SCMs, graph learning

item.title: WeaveLA: Event Driven Cross-Subtask Latent Memory Weaving for Repetitive Robot Manipulation
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 45, Paper 40, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index, DOI or venue acceptance, open-source code, repository quality, public implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-16 in cs.CV and cs.RO.; Method is described as a frozen VLA backbone plus event-triggered latent memory routed into action generation.; Evaluation uses RoboMME with a pi_0.5 backbone and reports SwingXtimes N=3 success rising from 0% to 47.8%.; Abstract claims single-execution episodes remain unchanged and paired analysis localizes gains to causally dependent tasks.

TL;DR: WeaveLA adds event-triggered latent memory tokens to a frozen VLA policy so repetitive robot manipulation tasks can carry information from one completed subtask into the next action-generation step.

Problem: The paper studies brittleness in short-window Vision-Language-Action policies on multi-stage or repetitive manipulation tasks where later subtasks depend on information produced by earlier subtasks.

Method: It proposes WeaveLA, a cross-subtask memory interface layered on a frozen VLA backbone: after each sub-goal completion event, the completed segment is compressed into latent tokens using query-driven attention pooling, then routed into the action-generation path for the next subtask.

Key contribution: The novelty claimed in the abstract is an explicit subtask-to-subtask hand-off at sub-goal completion events, injected on the action side, rather than frame-level memory writing, demonstration-stage retrieval, or event-triggered memory without direct action-expert routing.

Main result: On RoboMME with a pi_0.5 backbone, WeaveLA improves the hardest repetition slice, SwingXtimes with N=3, from 0% to 47.8% success, while single-execution episodes remain unchanged; paired analysis attributes gains to tasks requiring cross-subtask information.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, ablations, broader benchmarks beyond RoboMME, robustness tests, statistical confidence intervals, or real-robot validation; the result may be specific to repetitive tasks with clear sub-goal completion events.

Relevance: The matched query is causality, and the paper explicitly targets tasks whose causal structure requires information transfer across subtasks, making it relevant despite being primarily a robotics/VLA memory paper rather than a causal inference paper.

Tags: vision-language-action, robot manipulation, latent memory, cross-subtask reasoning, event-triggered memory, causal structure, RoboMME

Figure note: A useful figure would show the WeaveLA pipeline: completed subtask segment -> query-driven attention pooling -> latent memory tokens -> action-generation path for the next subtask on top of a frozen VLA backbone.

TL;DR: MODE-RAG proposes a dynamically gated multi-agent framework for reducing cross-modal hallucinations, causal fabrications, and sycophancy in multimodal retrieval-augmented generation.

Problem: The paper studies hallucination failure modes in M-RAG systems, especially cross-modal mismatches, causal fabrications, and sycophantic generations that can cascade when multimodal reasoning is left unguided.

Method: It proposes MODE-RAG, a multi-agent system using Variational Free Energy and internal attention states to decide when to intervene; high-risk queries are routed through five stage-specific agents, with MCTS for causal derivation, logit perturbations to penalize sycophancy, and Correction/Overseer agents for formatting stability and post-hoc factual verification.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is dynamic intervention gating for M-RAG hallucination mitigation, combining VFE/attention-based risk diagnosis with agent routing, MCTS-based causal reasoning, and sycophancy-targeted logit perturbation rather than relying on static rules or fully unguided generation.

Main result: The abstract reports extensive experiments showing reduced hallucination rates and logical fabrication on ModeVent, a challenging subset derived from MultiVent, but it does not provide numerical gains, baselines, or statistical details.

Limitation: The supplied metadata/abstract does not disclose exact metrics, baseline systems, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, data release details, or implementation specifics; the effectiveness claim is therefore not independently assessable from the abstract alone.

item.title: A Closer Look at Failure Modes in Temporal Understanding of Large Audio-Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 72, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index signals not supplied., DOI and journal reference absent., Code availability not supplied., Public data or benchmark release status not supplied., Evaluated model list not specified in the abstract.

Score evidence: Accepted to Interspeech 2026.; arXiv version is v1, published 2026-06-16.; Introduces a benchmark with 1,657 questions across three foundational tasks.; Reports causal mechanistic analysis of temporal reasoning failures in LALMs.

TL;DR: The paper studies why Large Audio Language Models fail at temporal audio reasoning and introduces a benchmark plus causal attention-based mechanistic analyses to diagnose and partially improve those failures.

Problem: LALMs perform well on many audio understanding tasks but struggle with temporal reasoning, and existing benchmarks mainly expose performance gaps without explaining the mechanisms behind the failures.

Method: The authors introduce a 1,657-question benchmark across three temporal reasoning tasks, run behavioral analyses under varying input settings, and perform causal mechanistic interventions on attention over audio tokens, including attention upweighting, scaling, bottleneck-layer targeting, and task-relevant token targeting.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is a benchmark designed for mechanistic analysis of LALM temporal reasoning plus a claimed first causal mechanistic analysis showing that redistributing attention across audio tokens can matter more than simply increasing audio attention.

Main result: Attention scaling at bottleneck layers improves accuracy from 55.9% to 59.1% without fine-tuning; the abstract also reports that models often under-use audio when textual cues are available and that task-relevant token targeting yields further gains.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the evaluated models, datasets beyond the new benchmark, baselines, ablations, statistical significance, robustness checks, code release, or whether the benchmark/data will be public.

item.title: Visuals Lie, Consistency Speaks: Disentangling Spatial Attention from Reliability in Vision-Language Models
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 55, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Author citation or h-index signals not supplied., No DOI or journal reference supplied., Dataset names and scenario coverage not specified in the abstract., Baselines against existing reliability methods not specified., Ablations and statistical robustness not specified.

Score evidence: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Multimodal Intelligence.; Code link is provided in the arXiv comment.; Abstract reports a systematic cross-family VLM study.; Abstract gives quantitative correlations: spatial attention R approximately 0.001 with accuracy; self-consistency R = 0.429.

TL;DR: The paper argues that spatial attention maps are poor reliability signals for contemporary VLMs, while generation-time self-consistency and hidden-state probes better predict truthful answers.

Problem: It studies how to detect when multimodal foundation models are reliable or hallucinating, especially whether visual attention/grounding patterns can serve as confidence signals.

Method: The authors propose the VLM Reliability Probe (VRP), a cross-family study using structural-attention metrics such as cluster counts, spatial entropy, layerwise entropy change, self-consistency across sampled reasoning paths, hidden-state probes, and causal interventions on model layers.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim from the abstract is a direct challenge to the Attention-Confidence Assumption: visual attention concentration is empirically detached from answer correctness, and reliability is instead tied to generation dynamics and internal-state distributions.

Main result: Spatial attention reportedly has near-zero correlation with accuracy (R approximately 0.001), while self-consistency is the strongest reported predictor of truth (R = 0.429). Causal interventions also reveal architectural differences between LLaVA and PaliGemma/Qwen2-VL.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, task coverage, baselines against existing reliability estimators, statistical uncertainty, ablations, or whether the released code fully reproduces all experiments.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it uses causal interventions to test where reliability information is represented inside VLM architectures, though its primary focus is VLM reliability rather than causal inference theory.

Tags: vision-language-models, reliability, hallucination-detection, attention-analysis, self-consistency, causal-interventions, hidden-state-probing

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's VRP pipeline or architecture/intervention figure showing attention metrics, hidden-state probes, self-consistency sampling, and causal layer interventions across VLM families.

item.title: Transformer-Based Warm-Starting for Feasible and Optimal Terminal Approach to Tumbling Objects with Space Manipulators
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 35, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI, venue, or journal reference, open-source code repository, public dataset or scenario generator, full baseline list

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-15 with no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Abstract reports evaluation across 300 held-out scenarios.; Abstract compares linear and flow-matching action decoders under different action chunking and training dataset sizes.; Reported gains include up to 28% fewer second-stage SCP iterations and 23% lower runtime.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causal-transformer warm-start for the computational bottleneck in sequential convex programming for terminal approach trajectories of space manipulators toward tumbling targets, reporting up to 23% runtime reduction on held-out scenarios.

Problem: Real-time trajectory generation for on-orbit robotic servicing is difficult because spacecraft bus motion, manipulator dynamics, visibility constraints, and trajectory-level safety constraints are nonlinearly coupled.

Method: The framework splits planning into a system center-of-mass translational planning stage and a coupled attitude-manipulator torque-allocation stage, then applies a causal transformer warm-start to the second SCP stage; linear and flow-matching action decoders are compared across action chunking and training-set sizes.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is using sequence-model warm-starts, specifically a causal transformer, to accelerate and stabilize the dominant SCP stage for space-manipulator terminal guidance rather than relying on heuristic initialization alone.

Main result: Across 300 held-out scenarios, learned warm-starts reduce second-stage SCP iterations by up to 28% and runtime by 23% while preserving the final control-cost distribution; for nonconvex feasibility projection, they nearly halve runtime relative to cost-optimal SCP and avoid high-cost tails seen with heuristic initialization.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention open-source code, public datasets, statistical uncertainty, detailed baselines beyond heuristic initialization, robustness tests outside the 300 scenarios, or real hardware/flight validation.

Relevance: The paper matched the query through the phrase 'causal transformer'; it is relevant if the feed is tracking causal sequence models or transformer-based planning, but less relevant if the intended topic is causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: robotics, space robotics, trajectory optimization, sequential convex programming, transformer, warm-starting, optimal control

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework/pipeline figure showing the two-stage planning decomposition and where the causal-transformer warm-start feeds into the attitude-manipulator SCP stage.

item.title: Kernel-Based Functional Balancing for Causal Inference with Compositional Treatments
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 55, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 40, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index information, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, open-source code, public data availability, specific datasets

Score evidence: arXiv v2 paper in stat.ME and stat.ML, updated 2026-06-17.; Abstract explicitly targets causal effect estimation with compositional treatments on a simplex.; Method uses RKHS worst-case balancing error rather than a treatment assignment model.; Claims sqrt(n)-consistency and asymptotic normality around a sample-specific target.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a kernel-based functional balancing and augmented weighted estimation framework for causal effect estimation when treatments are compositional vectors on a simplex.

Problem: It studies causal effect estimation for compositional treatments, where exposure values are proportions constrained to a simplex rather than binary, scalar, or unconstrained multivariate treatments.

Method: The authors construct weights by minimizing worst-case covariate-treatment balancing error over an RKHS on the joint treatment-covariate space, then combine these weights with kernel ridge regression outcome estimation in an augmented weighted estimator. They formulate the estimator as a finite-dimensional convex optimization problem using a representer theorem and low-rank approximation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is extending functional balancing to compositional treatment spaces and avoiding explicit treatment-assignment model estimation while still claiming root-n consistency for an augmented estimator.

Main result: The abstract claims sqrt(n)-consistency without requiring consistent weight estimation or smoothness of the weights, plus asymptotic normality around a sample-specific target. Empirical evidence is said to include simulations and a real data application, but no quantitative results are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report baselines, effect sizes, datasets, code availability, robustness checks, or how strong the real-data evidence is. The asymptotic normality target is sample-specific, which may limit direct interpretability relative to a fixed population estimand.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured causal inference query and is especially relevant if the researcher tracks nonstandard treatment regimes, continuous/multivariate exposures, RKHS balancing, or causal estimands for compositional data.

Tags: causal inference, compositional treatments, kernel methods, covariate balancing, RKHS, augmented weighted estimator, semiparametric estimation

Figure note: The paper likely contains useful figures because the metadata reports 3 figures; a pipeline diagram showing RKHS balancing weight construction followed by augmented weighted estimation would be the most useful image, but no direct image URL is supplied.

item.title: Semiparametric Mediation Analysis with Separately Observed Mediator and Outcome under Unmeasured Confounding
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 42, Paper 50, Empirical 50, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent from supplied metadata., Citation count and h-index absent., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or data availability statement supplied., No simulation metrics, baselines, ablations, or robustness results visible in the abstract., No quantitative empirical result for the dementia application in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in stat.ME, published 2026-06-15.; Abstract states mediator M and outcome Y are never jointly observed in the target setting.; Uses shared instrumental variables and gives separate strategies for known and learned valid IVs.; Claims semiparametric influence-function estimators with multiple robustness and efficiency-bound attainment.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causal data-fusion framework for mediation analysis when the mediator and outcome are observed in separate datasets, using shared instrumental variables to identify natural direct and indirect effects under unmeasured confounding.

Problem: Standard mediation analysis for natural direct and indirect effects typically requires joint observation of mediator M and outcome Y, but many real-world studies observe M in one source and Y in another, breaking the usual identification strategy.

Method: The paper introduces a semiparametric data-fusion framework that combines two incomplete data sources through shared instrumental variables, supports known-IV and learned-IV settings, handles unmeasured confounding under a no-interaction condition, and allows covariate/exposure shifts under a latent alignment condition.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is identification and efficient estimation of mediation effects without joint M,Y observation, using shared IV structure plus data-source alignment assumptions rather than complete-case or standard mediation identification.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: two identification strategies, multiply robust influence-function-based estimators, and an estimator attaining the semiparametric efficiency bound under suitable conditions. The paper also reports an application to SNP rs610932, dementia risk, and immune-related gene-expression mediation, but the abstract gives no quantitative effect estimate.

Limitation: The abstract relies on strong assumptions including valid shared IVs, no-interaction, and latent alignment; it does not report simulation results, baselines, robustness checks, code, data availability, or quantitative findings from the dementia application.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it targets causal mediation, unmeasured confounding, IV-based identification, and semiparametric causal effect estimation under incomplete data fusion.

Tags: causality, mediation-analysis, data-fusion, instrumental-variables, semiparametric-estimation, unmeasured-confounding, statistics

Figure note: The paper metadata says it contains 2 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied. A useful image would likely be the framework or causal graph showing two data sources, shared IVs, mediator M, outcome Y, and the data-fusion identification pipeline.

item.title: Statistical Foundations of LLM-based A/B Testing: A Surrogacy Framework for Human Causal Inference
item.description:
Priority: 59/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 52, Paper 60, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent from supplied metadata., Citation count and h-index absent., No DOI, journal, or conference venue listed., No code repository mentioned., No public data or replication package mentioned., No detailed LLM choice, prompt, temperature, or implementation details in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v2 paper published 2026-06-15 and updated 2026-06-18.; Categories include stat.ME, cs.AI, econ.EM, and math.ST.; Abstract states a statistical framework adapting surrogate endpoint theory to LLMs.; Abstract reports a falsification test, worst-case bias bound, simulations, and an Upworthy headline A/B-test application.

TL;DR: The paper develops a causal/statistical surrogacy framework for using LLM outputs in place of human outcomes in A/B tests, showing when calibrated LLM responses can identify human average treatment effects and when they fail.

Problem: It studies whether treatment effects estimated from LLM-simulated outcomes can recover the effects that would have been measured on a target human population in A/B testing.

Method: The authors adapt surrogate endpoint theory to LLM-based experimentation, deriving identification conditions based on surrogacy and comparability, adding a falsification test for surrogacy, a worst-case bias bound for limited LLM-human overlap, and an analysis of LLM stochasticity and repeated draws.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is framing LLM-based A/B testing as a surrogate-outcome causal inference problem rather than assuming distributional equivalence between LLM and human responses; this yields weaker but explicit assumptions, diagnostics, bias bounds, and calibration procedures.

Main result: According to the abstract, simulations validate the theoretical results, and an Upworthy headline A/B-test application finds raw LLM predictions recover only 39% of the human treatment effect, while nonparametric calibration closes the gap.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, datasets, model details, exact prompts, baselines beyond raw LLM predictions, robustness checks across LLMs, or full empirical design details; the paper also emphasizes that the required assumptions may be hardest to justify in settings where LLM substitution is most attractive.

Relevance: This is highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it directly studies identification of human causal effects, surrogate outcomes, treatment-effect estimation, bias, overlap, and validation when replacing human experimental outcomes with LLM-generated outcomes.

Tags: causal inference, A/B testing, large language models, surrogate endpoints, treatment effects, calibration, statistical methodology

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing treatment assignment, human outcomes, LLM surrogate outcomes, calibration from human pilot data, identification assumptions, falsification testing, and bias from limited overlap.

item.title: The Value Axis: Language Models Encode Whether They're on the Right Track
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 45, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 65, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI or venue information, quantitative metrics, baselines, ablations

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-06-15.; Abstract names Qwen3-8B as the studied model.; Method constructs a value axis from synthetic, in-context reinforcement learning data.; Abstract reports causal activation steering effects on self-correction, verbosity, backtracking, and exploration.

TL;DR: The paper argues that Qwen3-8B contains a linear “value” direction encoding expected goal success, and that manipulating this direction causally changes confidence, self-correction, and exploration behavior.

Problem: It studies whether language models internally track the value of their current trajectory, meaning whether an ongoing strategy is likely to achieve the model’s goal.

Method: The authors use synthetic in-context reinforcement learning data to construct a linear value axis in Qwen3-8B activations, test whether the axis separates behavioral states, steer activations along the axis, examine DPO-induced changes, and apply the axis to selected in-the-wild settings.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that expected goal success is linearly encoded in model activations and causally modulates behaviors such as confidence, backtracking, verbosity, and exploration, rather than merely correlating with verbalized confidence.

Main result: From the abstract, the value axis distinguishes high vs. low verbalized confidence, rollouts with vs. without backtracking, and correct vs. corrupted code; steering high value suppresses self-correction, while steering low value induces backtracking and exploration.

Limitation: The abstract reports no quantitative effect sizes, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, or statistical tests; evidence appears centered on Qwen3-8B, so generality across models and tasks is unclear from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matches the query “causality” because it uses activation steering and DPO analyses to test whether an internal representation causally affects model behavior.

Tags: language-model-interpretability, activation-steering, causality, reinforcement-learning, DPO, confidence, Qwen3-8B

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper’s framework or pipeline figure showing how the value axis is constructed from activations, then used for probing, steering, DPO analysis, and in-the-wild evaluation.

item.title: Geometric Action Model for Robot Policy Learning
item.description:

TL;DR: GAM repurposes a pretrained geometric foundation model into a language-conditioned robot manipulation policy that predicts future 3D latent geometry and actions through one shared backbone.

Problem: The paper studies how generalist robot policies can follow language instructions while explicitly reasoning about 3D object, camera, and action interactions needed for contact-rich manipulation, instead of relying mainly on 2D image frames or 2D-derived latents.

Method: It proposes the Geometric Action Model, which splits a pretrained geometric foundation model at an intermediate layer: shallow layers encode observations, an inserted causal future predictor forecasts future latent tokens conditioned on language, proprioception, and action history, and the remaining backbone blocks propagate features and decode future geometry plus robot actions.

Key contribution: The main novelty is using one pretrained geometric foundation model as a shared substrate for perception, temporal world modeling, and action decoding with minimal architectural modification, preserving geometric priors while adding language-conditioned future prediction.

Main result: The abstract claims GAM is more accurate, more robust, faster, and lighter than foundation-model-scale baselines across simulation and real-robot manipulation benchmarks, but it does not provide quantitative metrics or named benchmarks.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, exact metrics, baseline identities, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or failure cases, so empirical strength cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The match to the configured query "causality" appears mostly through the paper's use of a causal future predictor and physical action-effect modeling, not through causal inference or explicit causal discovery; relevance is therefore moderate to weak unless the feed includes causal world models for robotics.

Tags: robotics, vision-language-action, world models, 3D geometry, manipulation, foundation models, causal prediction

Figure note: A useful image would be the GAM architecture/pipeline figure showing the GFM split point, observation encoder, causal future predictor, language/proprioception/action-history conditioning, and downstream geometry/action decoding.

Problem: The paper studies how to identify causally meaningful heterogeneous treatment effects when important effect modifiers are unmeasured, causing both interpretable and expressive HTE methods to produce spurious subgroup characterizations.

Method: It proposes Neural EXposure Interaction Search (NEXIS), an iterative Markov-blanket discovery procedure applied to sufficient, aligned pre-treatment representations, including multimodal and multi-view inputs such as satellite imagery.

Key contribution: The novelty is the causal reframing of HTE characterization around latent interactors discovered from scalable pre-treatment representations, with a claimed consistent selection procedure rather than a standard predictive HTE or post-hoc subgroup analysis approach.

Main result: From the abstract, NEXIS is claimed to have provable and empirically validated consistent selection and is deployed on two anti-poverty programs in Africa, where satellite imagery reveals previously unmeasured environmental effect modifiers and yields interpretable policy guidelines. No quantitative performance numbers are reported in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide baselines, metrics, ablations, robustness checks, code availability, data availability, or quantified policy gains, so the empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be judged from the supplied metadata.

item.description:

TL;DR: DreamX-World 1.0 proposes an interactive text/image-to-video world model for controllable long-horizon generation with camera navigation, scene persistence, event control, and few-step autoregressive rollout.

Problem: The paper studies how to build a general-purpose video world model that can generate long-horizon interactive scenes while preserving camera control, revisiting previously seen regions, and responding to promptable events across varied visual domains.

Method: The system combines a multi-source data engine, E-PRoPE camera-aware positional encoding, causal forcing to convert a bidirectional video generator into an autoregressive world model, DMD-style distillation, long-rollout training on self-generated contexts, camera-geometry-based memory retrieval, residual recycling, event instruction tuning, RL alignment, and runtime optimizations for fast inference.

Key contribution: The most distinctive contribution is a full interactive world-model pipeline that combines camera-geometry-aware control, memory-conditioned scene persistence, event-level instruction control, and long-rollout self-conditioning to reduce drift in autoregressive video generation.

Main result: On a 5-second basic evaluation, DreamX-World 1.0 reports a camera-control score of 73.75 and overall score of 84.76, outperforming HY-WorldPlay 1.5 at 80.79 and LingBot-World at 80.45 overall; it also reports up to 16 FPS on eight RTX 5090 GPUs.

Limitation: The abstract does not report ablations, statistical robustness, dataset release details, human evaluation, long-duration benchmark results beyond the stated 5-second evaluation, or independent reproduction evidence.

Relevance: The match to the query "causality" appears mostly indirect: the abstract mentions "causal forcing" as a training mechanism for autoregressive rollout, but the paper is primarily about controllable video world models rather than causal inference or causality research.

Tags: world-models, text-to-video, image-to-video, camera-control, autoregressive-generation, DiT, scene-persistence, causal-forcing

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causal, model-agnostic empirical auditing framework for detecting whether synthetic data generators disclose private training records rather than merely producing coincidental matches.

Problem: It studies how to audit privacy leakage in synthetic data generated by LLMs or other generative systems, especially distinguishing real memorization-driven disclosure from incidental generation of the same information.

Method: The framework partitions data into training and holdout sets, compares synthetic outputs against held-out controls, and applies statistical hypothesis testing against privacy baselines such as zero-learning or specified differential privacy bounds. It requires only generated synthetic data and a holdout set, not model access, canary insertion, shadow models, or reference model training.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a causal framing that separates true disclosures from phantom disclosures, turning the audit into a membership-inference-style test that can provide empirical lower bounds on privacy leakage with much lower computational cost than canary- or shadow-model-based audits.

Main result: From the abstract, the authors report tighter empirical lower bounds on privacy leakage than prior data-based auditing methods and orders-of-magnitude lower computational requirements, but the abstract does not specify datasets, metrics, or numerical effect sizes.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide concrete experimental settings, numerical results, code availability, datasets, robustness checks, or ablations, so the strength and generality of the empirical claims cannot be assessed from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it explicitly frames synthetic-data privacy auditing causally, using the causal distinction between true and phantom disclosures as the basis for statistical auditing.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a causal-origin taxonomy for distributional shift in reinforcement learning, unifying ID/OOD generalization and non-stationarity as structured changes in the agent-environment generative process.

Problem: RL agents often degrade under distributional shift, but the abstract argues that the formal relationship between ID/OOD generalization, non-stationarity, and the causal origins of shift in agent-environment interaction remains unclear.

Method: The paper reformulates dataset shift for RL using a POMDP-based generative interaction process, decomposing shift sources across state distribution, observation process, policy, reward, transition dynamics, and a shifted-time boundary. It also proposes an evaluation framework based on performance degradation and recovery metrics.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a unified causal-origin taxonomy that distinguishes internal agent-driven shifts from external environment-driven shifts and characterizes explicit, implicit, and hybrid shifts via a shifted-time boundary perspective.

Main result: No empirical result is reported in the abstract; the stated contribution is conceptual and methodological, with an evaluation framework introduced but not described as experimentally validated.

Limitation: The abstract does not report experiments, datasets, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, implementation details, code, or evidence that the proposed taxonomy improves analysis or adaptation in concrete RL benchmarks.
